title: New York Fed's Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit Shows Signs of Healing in Consumer Credit Markets Since Last Quarter

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The NY Fed released its Q1 2011 Quarterly Household Debt and Credit Report showing signs of healing in consumer credit markets, including the first increase in credit limits since Q3 2008 (up ~$30bn or 1%), continued decline in new foreclosures (-17.7%) and bankruptcies (-13.3%), and a 15% decline in total delinquent balances versus a year prior. Non-housing debt declined slightly with credit card balances dropping 4.6%.
key_points: Credit limits increased by approximately $30 billion (1%) for the first time since Q3 2008
New foreclosures declined 17.7% and new bankruptcies declined 13.3% quarter-over-quarter
Total delinquent balances fell 15% compared to one year prior
Non-housing debt (credit cards, student loans, auto loans) declined less than 1%, driven by 4.6% credit card balance decline
Number of open mortgage accounts stabilized following decline that began in early 2008
Credit inquiries declined from their Q4 2010 peak, indicating reduced consumer demand for new credit
why_it_matters: The report provides insight into household sector deleveraging and credit market healing following the 2008 financial crisis. These trends are relevant to understanding credit market structure and consumer credit conditions, though the data is historical (2011) and does not reflect current market conditions.
macro_context: The report indicates gradual healing in consumer credit markets as households continued deleveraging post-2008 crisis, with reduced delinquencies and bankruptcies suggesting improving household financial health.
market_structure_context: The report documents changes in credit market structure including: increase in available credit (credit limits), stabilization of mortgage accounts, decline in distressed outcomes (foreclosures, bankruptcies), and shifting composition of household debt. These are structural indicators of credit market health.
important_numbers: $30 billion increase in credit limits (1% increase)
15% year-over-year decline in total delinquent balances
less than 1% decline in non-housing debt
